sentences.text: Yes.
They tried to destroy Peenemunde because of the V bomb, V-2 bomb.
They bombed mostly the places where the construction of the V bomb were going to be.
And also the railroads.
They wanted to destroy the railroads so no shipments can come in and come back.
In our camps, we were about-- we-- we had about 12 bombs who dropped in our camps, and mostly in the area of the construction of the V bomb.
But like everybody else when bomb falls, and you hear the noise, you get scared.
And some people started to run away from their bunker or from wherever they were lodging.
And most of these people who run away from the place where we were located were all killed, because the bombs actually fell around the camp, not inside the camp.
So we lost about 20 people, and that was really hard to-- to see that the people dying that way, besides we see-- saw people dying the other way, you know.
No, we had-- I had no friends dying.
[INTERPOSING VOICES] We were very fortunate, yeah.
From-- from the Dora we went on the-- I mean, from Peenemundee-- we went back to Buchenwald.
In order to get from Peenemundee, they had to go on barges to trains.
Because it was in-- in the North Sea and was like and island.
So that's-- that's why we had to go on barges.
And when we got back to Buchenwald, we stayed there for about two weeks.
They just let us recuperate a little bit from the bombing.
And then they shipped us to Camp Dora.
Camp Dora-- Buchenwald is here, Camp Dora was about 60 to 70 kilometer north of--
Buchenwald.
No, no, no, no.
Because we all had still our prisoner clothes on, and then-- you know, the-- they kept us separate, because we came from that same-- transport from Peenemundee, so they kept us in separate barracks.
And we-- I left-- that was in April of '43.
Yes.
To rebuild a new camp for the V-2.
Camp Dora was a camp-- an underground camp, where in 19-- in the First World War, the German had built tunnels through that mountain.
I don't know if it was for train, or whatever it was.
So there were two tunnels, one going-- on both sides there were two big tunnels.
So they wanted to use this, and there was a big mountain, a mountain where nobody could bomb.
If they had try and bombed it, you couldn't penetrate into the-- to-- to the-- to the factory.
But in order to manufacture that V-2, they didn't have enough tunnels.
So we had to dig new tunnels.
They only had about two tunnels going across from one side to the other, so they needed about 12 tunnels in order to go in production of the V-2.
So we had to dynamite the new tunnels.
In that way we lost a lot of people.
A lot of prisoners of war, Russian prisoners who came there from Czechoslovakia-- I mean they were not doing what they're supposed to do with prisoner.
They used the prisoner as workers, not as prisoners, the Germans.
My brother was always with me.
My brother and I stayed all-- we were very fortunate to always stay together.
In Dora, first were terrible, because we had to sleep in the tunnel.
We had one area where they-- where we-- where we were sleeping.
Moist, [INAUDIBLE]-- everything was terrible there.
The-- you didn't get out of the tunnel.
You didn't get any fresh air.
Once in a while they took us out for half an hour to assemble us outside to count us, and-- and that's about-- that's about all.
And then when the time did come, they built a camp outside the tunnel.
But in beginning, until we had the tunnel done for manufacturing, most of us had to sleep inside the tunnel.
Only--
Just electrical light.
That's all.
No fresh air.
All-- all moisture.
And then all the dynamite smell you had from the-- from-- blowing out the-- the tunnels.
Oh, the housing that was inside, they had-- I told you, they had two tunnels already.
In those tunnels we had to sleep.
In the rest, we had then to-- to manufacture new tunnels.
And in-- in-- in the same time, they were building outside barracks to house everybody, because they needed the tunnels to manufacture the V-2.
That was in-- in April-- April, May of '43.
And when we finished the tunnels, we-- Buchenwald was sending us every two weeks 1,000 people.
And we also, in two weeks, we sent back to Buchenwald 1,000 dead ones, because, you know, the humidity, the explosion, and people were dying.
Hunger, starving, and everything.
I was-- we were very fortunate that we always could-- could survive, my brother and I.
We were stealing.
I mean, really could say stealing.
If you saw somebody dying, and was dead, you stole his piece of bread to survive.
When you-- when you're prisoner, you do any-- do anything to survive.
And then when the kitchen-- in the soup kitchen came down, we-- if we could get an extra bowl of soup, we could try to get an extra bowl of soup.
But we-- we tried to survive.
And then, once the tunnel went under, we made 10 tunnels, even with the [POINTS] cross tunnels.
Then we were building two tunnels about 50 feet deeper than the regular tunnels.
Because when the V-2 came through, on the end, they would not lay on it, they would stand 'em up to check them out if the-- if everything was working.
So in '40-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] in beginning '44, we were all at the camp outside, and the tunnel was finished, and we were starting to produce-- produce the V-2.
Rockets?
Rocket.
So the rocket-- the rockets would come in, the-- the material, and then we would put-- assemble them.
And each tunnel had a different work to do on-- on the rocket.
One was working on the motors.
One was working on the-- excuse me-- on the shell-- you know, they had the shell that had the propane gas tank would go in.
We had to assemble those.
Then we had to assemble the wings on the-- on the rockets.
And then the motor came onto the last.
And I was working mostly where the motor was being put together.
And we would do a lot of sabotage there.
On the-- because we would tell amongst us, French people, or even amongst the others, don't put the red wire where the red dot-- knot is.
Put the yellow wire on.
Because, you know, everything was marked for us.
Because they couldn't speak the language, so they would mark it in color scheme.
So we would put the red wire with a yellow knot, or the yellow wire with a red knot, so that-- that that would be not functioning properly.
And sometimes we had guards behind us, because we had started it, and they [? noticed ?] what was going on.

So on the-- then on the end, when the rocket was being finished up, they would throw in wires, or any kind of tools, or anything, just to offset the-- because the rocket at the time was radar-controlled, though not like today.
In those days, they was-- they were still radar-controlled.
So we-- we would offset it.

When that happened, after a while they were getting very disgusted, because we were-- we were manufacturing about 20-- 20 rockets a day, and sometimes 10, 12 would come back two-- two or three day later, because we had to do them all over again.
So in order to scare us, and to make sure that we don't do any more sabotage, when we would walk out of the tunnel, they would take 10 people at random and hang them in front of us.
Or they would do it in the evening at assembly, when they were counting.
They would count us almost every day, because they know people were going to run away, or something would happen.

Then they would-- on the assembly line, they would put two big posts with another post, and then they would put 10, 12-- 10 people there with a-- with a bench underneath and a [? stool. ?] They counted one, two, three, and they would hang them, just to scare us so that we wouldn't do any more sabotage.
They know, they felt, this is the only way they could prevent people of doing things they shouldn't do, because they didn't know if you were the next, or you were the next.
You never knew who was the next to be killed.
We had to be very careful.
We had to be very careful how we could talked.
We could talk to each other, but not to-- to a group, because you never knew if there was somebody listening, or if there was an informer or something in there.
In 19-- when-- when all that sabotage went on, we had a good friend who was a Catholic priest.
And being a priest, and being more educated, they figured he had something to do of the sabotaging.
So one evening, they took him out on-- in front of the assembly, put him on a bench, and gave him 10 slashes on his back with a hot iron.
Now, can you figure out what that-- what that is?
A hot iron?
Opened up almost his whole back.
And that was just before the end of the war.
And you witnessed that?
You saw that?
Yeah.
Mhm.
When we came back to France, naturally, being a priest, he got every attention.
He had the best doctors in Paris all over to give him surgeries, plastic surgeries.
Nothing helped.
After two years, he died.
And that was a young man.
He was about 30, 35 years old when he was, I think.
I had-- I used to have a book of his.
He wrote a book before he died.
My-- my-- my brother has it, but it's in French.
So.
And he didn't know where we went, but he mentioned our name.
Beaumont.
Isn't that amazing?
He didn't know where we went and where we stayed and what-- what happened to us, [INTERPOSING VOICES] but he did mention our name in that book-- Beaumont, you see.
And that was-- Camp Dora was one of the roughest, roughest camps you could believe.
In 1944, in January of 1944, we got a lot of Jewish, Jews, coming in from Auschwitz to help us, because the Russian was coming in.
So they evacuated a lot of Jews.
So I had-- we had the young kids-- eight, nine years old.
And I took one of the kids, to-- to give them food.
But I couldn't tell them that I was a Jew, [LAUGHS] you know?
What can you do?
You-- you know what they are, but I could not really help tell him that I was one of-- of our kind.
So we helped-- I helped them.
Did you get more food than the Jewish prisoners?
Did you get more--
It's not that we got more food, but like I said, my brother and I, we were already a little more advanced, and I had more advantages already.
I will show you later on.
I had an advantage.
I could grow my hair.
I became more like a-- not-- like a foreman in the-- in the-- in Dora, because I spoke French, and so I would translate, so they gave me more permission.
Then I had to run some paperwork from one area to the office of Wernher von Braun.
So they gave me the permission of growing my hair-- you saw that one paper I showed you.
And then they have-- had a paper to show that I was allowed to go from one area of the factory to another area of the factory.
It's all-- all the papers, I still have those.
But I did-- we did not have advantage of food.
The only thing is, I worked one time in the kitchen, and I worked there for about three or four weeks, and some of my friends were hungry, so I put meat and stuff in my clothes, and I got caught.
So I had to go back in the factory again.
So, you know, but-- but those things happened.
You do-- you're not only trying to survive yourself.
When you have good friends, you're trying to help them, too.
So that's-- that's one part I-- I-- we did-- we did do.
Did you witness a lot of mistreatment?
Oh, yes.
Yes.
We saw people slashed, killed, because they didn't do the work.
I saw people dying one after another, because of famine.
I-- one time I slept amongst the dead, because there was supposed to be a transport going out of Dora to another camp, but I didn't want to leave yet Dora.
So we slept amongst that dead, and one transport went out, and we were still alive.
But we-- I-- I helped bury people.
I mean, saw people to throw them on piles.
I-- I had to help.
At one time, I even had to help pulling teeth, the gold teeth, out of dead people.
What kept you going is you always think you're going to make it.
You have to make it.
You're going to make it, and you have to make it, and you're going to make it.
And, you know, being my brother and I together helped each other.
We-- we could-- when one morale-- when one's morale was down, then the other one has-- had to boost the other one.
When the other one-- it reversed itself.
So we had to keep ourself always in-- And we have to say, we-- my mother always raised us very, very strong.
She didn't put any-- she made us work hard and everything else, and I believed that has helped.
The exercise, the being in a family where you had to work, and-- not because we needed it all the time, but we had to work, regardless, from morning to night.
And I think it makes you body stronger.
So Dora was one of the worst camp.
And then in 19-- in April of '45, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] suddenly-- suddenly a word came down that the-- the Russian were advancing, the American were advancing.
Going back, when the American landed in France, in 1944--
That was the worst time in the war.
Because they knew something was going to happen.
They let it out on us.
They were mean.
Meaner and meaner.
The Germans.
The SS.
And they had a lot of older Germans who were trying not to be so mean, but the younger generation made them do things when they didn't want to.
So we-- we suffered.

I mean, that rationing were cut, the-- the work was put on stronger, more and more work were put on you, because they wanted that V-2 to-- to destroy England, so that the American could not land in England and-- and come to the United-- to-- to Europe.
So it was very, very difficult at the time.
But we survived.
You survived.
[LAUGHS] In 19-- then in April of [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] '45, the-- the American were not far away from Buchenwald and Dora, so they tried to evacuate-- evacuate us again to-- to another camp.
They put us again in the boxcars and shipped us out.
And then suddenly, we-- one night, we hear the Russians coming from this side, the Americans from this side, and the-- we saw the Germans leaving.
So we knew something was going to happen.
The SS and the German soldiers-- they were leaving the train, because they didn't want to get caught with the uniform on, some of them.
So my brother and six other friends, we took off.
We opened up the box-- boxcar and took off.
It was during the night, and we were not far away from the farm.
And we said, let's go and stay over night in the farm.
We went into the farm, went to the barn, and-- and slept in in the hay there.
Where was this farm, approximately?
Was it--
That's near-- near the Alp.
Yeah.
So we went into the farm, and the-- that morning, because I spoke pretty good German, I went to the farmer, and I said, can you see who we are?
We are prisoner of concentration camp.
We need food, and we need clothes.
We want to run away from the Germans.
And they didn't give us any problems.
They knew what was going to happen.
They gave us clothes, they fed us, and the seven of us, we took off.
And we start walking, because the Russian were coming, and we didn't want to be in a Russian zone.
The French, the others were coming the other way.
We didn't want to be in the Russian zone.
So we took off, and we marched and marched.
And suddenly, we come to the Alp.
And there were French, American, and English soldiers standing there.
And there was a bridge, and the Germans were coming.
And they were giving them their revolvers and their guns.
We were laughing about it then.
And they were throwing them into the-- to river, right and left.
You know.
So we kept on walking.
And suddenly, we saw a big-- a And suddenly, we saw French, English, and-- and [POINTING] the American flag-- flag.
And all seven of us start crying like children, [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] because we knew we were free.
And then we-- then we went to-- to-- to the department of the French governments, military government, and got our papers to-- to go home to France.
But when you knew you were free, what was going through your mind?
Can you-- what-- what--
It's very difficult to explain that.
It is such a happiness and such emotion that it just-- you can't-- it's really very difficult to explain or to tell anybody about it.
I mean, we-- we jumped in joy but-- and-- and-- and sang the French national anthem.
[LAUGHS] You know?
'Allons Enfants.' [LAUGHS] Yeah, that's-- that's one thing.
You know.
It's very hard to really describe freedom.
You know?
So then we went to-- to the French military.
And they gave us that fiche.
And that meant we can take any-- any transportation what-- what is available to go back to France.
And our--
It's just a [? clean ?] paper, and it says fiche on.
And that we showed then to-- to the-- to the train people.
You know, you had to pay from station to station.
So that gave them-- they knew that this meant that we were prisoners, and we are on our way home.
And we went back to Limoges and found our parents.
Did I mention that my sister was in a convent?
I forget.
I have it marked.
I don't remember.
I can't remember.
